Mapping the
Interactive
Experience

Building the platforms in parallel, journal, Instagram, AR, VR, and physical artifacts, and conducting the first round of user testing.

02 of 03
Development
Working Prototypes
March 11, 2026
PROJECT OVERVIEW

Concept to
prototype

Project 2 was the development phase, taking the narrative concept from the proposal and building actual, working prototypes across all five platforms simultaneously. The team divided labor by role: Marion led visual design and AR, Rabia led the digital journal and project management, Alejandra led the VR and physical artifacts.

The core challenge was maintaining aesthetic coherence across platforms that were built using completely different tools. A Spline 3D scene, an Instagram feed, a web journal, a physical toolbox, and an AR experience all needed to feel like they belonged to the same world.

AI played a significant role here for the first time in production, Claude was used to generate the entire journal website from natural language descriptions, eliminating the need for any team member to write code from scratch. This compressed what would have been days of development into hours.

DIGITAL JOURNAL

Building the
journal with AI

The journal at damons-end-journal.vercel.app is the primary narrative hub. It was built entirely using Claude AI, the team described the concept, visual aesthetic, and desired functions in direct natural language, and Claude generated a complete, functional web application.

The aesthetic direction: cyber-tech terminal with glitch effects, CRT scanlines, and neon glow. Six journal entries unlock progressively as the user advances through the story, preventing out-of-order access.

The journal also serves as a meta-layer of immersion, it references real Sheridan classroom numbers and names real IXD professors, grounding Damon as a believable student from the same program as the audience.

AI impact on development: Style changes after user testing, bolder fonts, stronger glow effects, less noise, were applied instantly through follow-up Claude prompts. The full build and iteration cycle that would normally take days of manual development was compressed into hours.

Journal Features Live at Vercel
  • Cyber-tech / terminal aesthetic with glitch effects, scanlines, neon glow, CRT visuals
  • 6 journal entries unlocking progressively, locked until preceding entry is viewed
  • Embedded clues link to Instagram, audio platform, and Scenery AR
  • Code puzzle gates the final end-of-game experience
  • Mysterious flavour text as red herring and atmospheric detail
  • References real IXD classroom numbers for immersive fourth-wall blur
  • Stored on GitHub, deployed and hosted on Vercel
Claude AI built No manual coding GitHub → Vercel
damons-end-journal.vercel.app
Next Steps (at time of P2)
  • Replace placeholder links with real Instagram, audio, AR, and VR URLs
  • Record Damon's audio with ElevenLabs AI and upload to audio platform
  • Replace journal content for full narrative accuracy and flow
  • End-to-end user testing of the complete rabbit hole before launch
02a //LIVE JOURNAL SCREENSHOT

Entry 05 of 06 — "The Signal." Data corruption detected. The archive capacity sits at 99.4%. A keyfragment is embedded in the entry text. The system log on the right tracks every user action in real time.

DAMON // RESTRICTED JOURNAL — ARCHIVE V0.9.2 ● CONNECTION: COMPROMISED
INSTAGRAM ACCOUNT

Building Damon's
social presence

The Instagram account (@damontorre.s) was designed to pass as a real person's profile, not a project asset. Every decision, from post timing to caption voice, was made to make Damon feel inhabited rather than constructed.

The process started with a 3×3 grid storyboard mapping each post to a specific beat in Damon's narrative arc. The grid was planned before anything went live, so the full shape of his descent was visible from the start.

The first three posts were made using a combination of Adobe Stock photography and original illustration, establishing his personality: enthusiastic about his work, warm with his community, grateful, and proud, but already a little secretive. Posts were spaced at realistic intervals rather than published all at once, to simulate a real posting cadence.

Friends and community were enlisted to follow, like, and comment, because Damon is meant to be a popular and respected person in his program. Real engagement made the account feel lived-in. A comment reading "Dude where are you" after his posts stop is more unsettling when it comes from a real person than from a test account.

As Damon's story takes a darker turn, images began to be processed with a glitch and noise effect, the visual language of the account degrades alongside his mental state. The formula for later posts: blend real photography, illustration, realistic captions, and audience participation at opportune moments in the timeline.

The account ends with a clearly identifiable rabbit hole, an easily spottable hidden code leading to the next step of the experience.

PRODUCTION PROCESS
Post by post
Posts 1–3: Adobe Stock + illustration. Establishes Damon as prominent, warm, passionate about his program. Presentation post thanks friends and profs. Cat birthday post humanises him.

Posts 4–6: Tone shifts. More focused on the work. Secretive captions. Physical computing content. Realistic timeframe between posts maintained.

Posts 7–9: Glitch and noise effects applied to images. Captions become more erratic. Final post appears to erase him from reality. Rabbit hole embedded and clearly identifiable.
USER TESTING RESULT
Character arc confirmed
Test users accurately read Damon as: studious, perfectionist, possibly struggling with depression, someone who highly prioritises academic success, and someone who wants to be perceived as approachable. This was the exact intended characterisation. The audience engagement, real comments, real reactions, validated the social platform as effective narrative territory.
Adobe Stock + illustration Glitch effects applied late arc Real audience engagement
instagram.com/damontorre.s
03a //STORYBOARD PROCESS WORK

Before a single post went live, the full nine-post arc was sketched as a 3×3 grid, one cell per post, each mapped to a narrative beat. The second sheet added captions and intent notes, specifying exactly what each post needs to establish about Damon.

SHEET 01, VISUAL SKETCHES
Visual sketches for all 9 posts: smiling Damon in the lab, wires and equipment, bare branches (isolation creeping in), a mushroom, his cat Cashew, books and papers, a presentation setup, a physical computing/soldering moment, and a skull, the full arc from warmth to erasure mapped before a single post went live.
SHEET 02, CAPTIONS + NARRATIVE INTENT
Caption sheet adds exact post copy and character intent: "Almost there." (secretive excitement), the Cashew birthday post ("establishes Damon as someone with a heart, with loved ones and depth outside of his program"), the presentation gratitude post ("establishes him as a prominent and respected name in the program who's serious about his work"), and the final descent posts.
AR EXPERIENCE

Finding the right
AR platform

The AR experience went through a full platform evaluation before a single frame was built. The first attempt was Snap AR, but it was unruly, difficult to get functioning reliably, and required the user to have Snapchat installed. That last point was a dealbreaker: adding a download step in the middle of an experience breaks immersion and loses users.

Other platforms were researched, ZapAR and Reality Composer were both evaluated. Ultimately Scenery Pro was selected: the interface was the most intuitive, it worked smoothly on iPad, required no external downloads, and had a face tracking feature that would become central to the final experience.

With the platform locked, the AR experience was conceptualised step-by-step from the narrative notes, identifying exactly what models and interactions would be needed for each stage: the loading moment, the consciousness theft, and the final error message.

3D models were sourced and selected based on how well they fit the project's tone and visual style. The error message was custom designed to match the overall aesthetic. Everything was assembled using Scenery's face tracking, animation, and timing/action features.

To handle a real UX concern, how does the user know to look into the camera?, a "ENSURE FACE IS VISIBLE" note was added at the start of the experience, worded to fit the tone of the world rather than read as a technical instruction.

User testing provided feedback on the exact sizing and wording of the error message, which was revised before final delivery. Multiple masks were tested during this phase, see the iterations below.

PLATFORM EVALUATION
Why Scenery Pro won
✗ Snap AR, First attempt. Unruly, unreliable, and required users to have Snapchat. Eliminated.

~ ZapAR, Evaluated. Not selected.

~ Reality Composer, Evaluated. Not selected.

✓ Scenery Pro, Selected. Most intuitive interface, smooth on iPad, no external downloads required, face tracking built in. Skills from Adobe Aero transferred directly.
EXPERIENCE STRUCTURE
Three stages
Stage 1, Loading: Innocuous loading symbol. User doesn't yet know what's coming.

Stage 2, Consciousness theft: The mask locks onto the face via face tracking. Something is wrong.

Stage 3, Error message: Custom-designed, undismissable. Button becomes pressable only at the very end. "THERE IS NOTHING YOU CAN DO."
Live (old experience):
scnry.app/cJfkqZE3o2b
04a //MASK ITERATIONS

Multiple masks were tested during development. Both use the same face tracking mechanics and the same error message, but the mask material, colour, and texture changed the feeling of the experience significantly.

ITERATION 01, GLOSSY PURPLE MASK
A smooth, high-gloss deep purple mask. Reflective and alien-looking, the surface has a distinctly dehumanising quality. The error message visible: "YOU ARE NO MORE AND HAVE JOINED DAMON AND ENZO IN THEIR FATE, ERASURE FROM REALITY IMMINENT" with the button "THERE IS NOTHING YOU CAN DO."
Tested Not selected
ITERATION 02, ORIGINAL MASK (SELECTED)
The original mask, dark, rough-textured, heavier. Photographed on iPad (Wed Apr 15, 9:47PM, day before the showcase). The error message is partially visible at top. The original mask was selected as the best version, it reads as erasure rather than transformation. It removes the face without replacing it with anything attractive.
Selected Final version
Design rationale: The glossy purple read as "sci-fi cool", aesthetically interesting but emotionally distancing. The original dark textured mask reads as something heavier: a face being covered, a self being lost. That's the feeling the ending needed to land.
VR EXPERIENCE

Building the
corrupted mind

The VR experience was built in Spline, a 3D web-based tool that allows interactive scenes to be deployed as URLs. The core goal: communicate that something is deeply wrong with the AI system, using the environment itself as the language of corruption.

The P2 approach was to modify an existing Spline community scene and adapt it to depict the corrupted AI mind. A separate Spline scene was adapted for an ESC button mechanic, the intended puzzle escape route. In P2, these were still two separate scenes; merging them was a stated next step.

The visual strategy: drastic contrast between green and red lighting to create a feeling of alarm. Tense music to reinforce the sense that something is wrong. The user should feel they have very little control.

Claude was also used in P2 to attempt to troubleshoot a Spline integration issue, though this particular attempt was not successfully implemented.

SCENE 01, CORRUPTED SYSTEM
The AI mind interior
Modified from an existing Spline community scene. Depicts the interior of the corrupted AI mind, retro-futuristic grid space with dramatic lighting. Green and red contrast communicates system malfunction. The user is inside something that is consuming them without their knowledge.
SCENE 02, RETROFUTURISTIC CIRCUIT LOOP
The escape mechanic
A separate Spline scene adapted from a community retrofuturistic circuit loop. Modified to resemble an ESC button and styled to match the retro-futuristic colour scheme. Experimented with linking the journal when the button is pressed. Next step at P2: integrate both scenes into one.
Spline 3D Community scenes adapted Two scenes, not yet merged
05a //SPLINE COMMUNITY TEMPLATES, P2 RESEARCH

These two Spline community projects were used as the foundation for the P2 VR concept, elements from both were combined and adapted to build the corrupted AI mind environment.

RETRO VIBE, by gikerek (Spline)
A retrofuturistic grid landscape with a setting sun. Used as a starting point for the VR's spatial aesthetic, the receding grid floor, dark atmosphere, and neon glow informed the environment structure.P2 foundation
RETROFUTURISTIC CIRCUIT LOOP, by DesignGabor (Spline)
A glowing circuit-board style 3D loop. Contributed the tech/circuit visual language and the cyan neon glow that became the project's signature VR colour palette.P2 foundation
PHYSICAL ARTIFACTS

The note and
the toolbox

The physical entry point was designed around one key insight: people are naturally nosy. The team wanted something accessible and eye-catching but different from a standard poster, and decided to capitalise on people's tendency to get into other people's business, combined with Damon's character as a bit of an eccentric guy.

The design process for the box went through several iterations. The first ideas had a cyberpunk style with buttons, lights, and cables, but the concern was that it looked too threatening and might be removed by security. Microsoft's AI datacentre racks were then researched to match the story's world more authentically, and a laser-cut miniature version was considered. This was dropped: a small rack lost its clear identity and could be mistaken for a locker, and a vertical structure wouldn't fit the candy and wires inside.

The final solution was Damon's abandoned red toolbox, the kind a physical computing student would actually carry. The red colour was chosen deliberately so it would stand out in a classroom. It was ordered online along with Pull-n-Peel Twizzlers (chosen after research into candies that resemble wires), and real alligator clip wires were borrowed from the classroom. The QR code was printed and everything assembled inside.

Alongside the box, a handwritten angry note was posted nearby, designed to look as legitimate as possible using simulated lined paper and red pencil, with big bold writing and wording that reads as a genuine call to action. It sparks the initial curiosity that brings people into the room.

ITERATION PROCESS
From cyberpunk to toolbox
✗ Cyberpunk box (buttons/lights/cables), Looked too threatening, might be removed by security.

✗ Laser-cut AI server rack, Too small to be identifiable; couldn't fit contents inside a vertical structure. Could be mistaken for a locker.

✓ Red metal toolbox, Damon's toolbox. Character-authentic, highly visible, large enough for contents. Ordered online.
FINAL CONTENTS
Assembled artifact
Red metal toolbox (Amazon) · Real alligator clip wires (borrowed from classroom) · Pull-n-Peel Twizzler bags (candy resembling wires) · Printed QR code · Post-it from Damon. Twizzlers were selected after specifically researching candies that look like wires. The candy reward made participation feel worth it.
06a //DESIGN RESEARCH + ITERATIONS

The physical artifact went through multiple concepting stages before landing on the toolbox. The images below trace the research and iteration path.

INSPIRATION, MYSTERY BOX CONCEPTS
Early ideation: cyberpunk-style cases with buttons, lights, and wires. The yellow mechanical case (ENEKILLER) and wire/candy reference images helped establish that the box should invite curiosity rather than look threatening. Among Us 'Fix Wiring' reference also explored for tone.Discarded direction
REFERENCE, MICROSOFT AI DATACENTRE
Real AI server rack from a Microsoft datacentre, researched to match the story world more authentically. A miniature laser-cut version was considered before being rejected: too small to be identifiable as an AI rack, and couldn't hold the contents.Research ref
ITERATION, LASER-CUT LOCKER
A laser-cut miniature locker explored as a physical container. Ultimately rejected: it looked like a generic locker rather than anything related to Damon's project, couldn't hold candy and wires inside a vertical structure, and lacked the visual impact of the red toolbox.Rejected
SELECTED, RED METAL TOOLBOX (AMAZON)
The final toolbox, a bright red metal tool case ordered online. 14.2" × 5.9" × 4.8". The red colour was chosen deliberately to stand out in the classroom. Damon's character as a physical computing student made this feel narratively authentic.✓ Selected
FINAL CONTENTS CHECKLIST
✓ Red metal toolbox (Amazon)
✓ Alligator clip wires (borrowed from classroom)
✓ Pull-n-Peel Twizzler bags (looks like wires)
✓ Printed QR code → Damon's journal
✓ Post-it note from Damon
Angry note: Simulated lined paper + red pencil. Big bold writing. Wording designed as a genuine call to action, angry enough to feel real, specific enough to send people to classroom AA1A.
AUDIENCE INTEGRATION

Making the
audience participants

Target audience: Sheridan IXD students. The integration strategy went beyond "find the experience", the goal was to make the audience feel like they're inside Damon's world, not observing it from outside.

In the Journal4th wall breaks
Damon mentions real IXD classroom numbers, creating a direct overlap between his world and the audience's. He's presented as an actual IXD student who went missing. The audience is investigating someone who could have been their peer. This personal proximity was the key to making the stakes feel real.
On InstagramParasocial arc
The audience perceives Damon's personality and state of mind through his posts. His arc moves from approachable to unsettling, and audience members who follow along in real time experience that shift as it happens. Real comments expressing concern made the account feel inhabited rather than constructed.
VR ExperienceActive confrontation
The VR shifts the audience from passive to active, they're not watching someone navigate the corrupted AI mind, they're inside it themselves, trying to escape. The puzzle forces a moment of genuine problem-solving before they can leave. This creates embodied stakes that watching a video or reading text cannot.
AR ExperiencePersonal ending
The AR artifact makes the audience witness Enzo's final fate, but also, through face tracking in the final version, makes them experience it directly. The mask locks over their face. The message tells them they've joined Damon. The ending happens to them, not to a character they're observing.
AI IMPACT IN P2

How AI changed
the workflow

CLAUDE, PRIMARY BUILD TOOL
Journal development
The journal website was described to Claude in direct language, aesthetic, function, structure. Claude generated a working web application. No team member wrote code from scratch. This was the first time in the project that AI moved from brainstorming support to primary builder. Rapid iteration via follow-up prompts replaced the normal design-dev cycle.
AI, CONTENT & WRITING
Tone + consistency
AI assisted with phrasing, grammar, and refining journal entries and project documentation throughout P2. Kept Damon's voice consistent across multiple entries written over weeks. Also used to research upcoming platforms, identified ElevenLabs as the right tool for AI voice generation for Damon's audio files in the next phase.
CLAUDE, TROUBLESHOOTING
Attempted Spline fix
Claude was used to attempt to troubleshoot a Spline integration issue during VR development. The brainstormed solution was ultimately not successfully implemented and was not carried forward. This is documented honestly. AI is a collaborator with limitations, not every prompt delivers a usable solution, and knowing when to move on is part of the process.
